description:
Grab your free cash flow planning template here: https://trybta.com/bmv

Want more tools, systems and expert advise? Check out Breakthrough Academy here: https://trybta.com/learn-more

We’ve all heard the statistics about how half of all businesses go under within 5 years.

But do you know why?

It's not because they can’t find customers or employees. It's not because they get into legal trouble. It's not even that they’re unprofitable.. 

According to a report published by US Bank, when businesses fail, it’s a direct result of mismanaged cash flow 82% of the time.

In other words, businesses die because they run into cash crunches.

They fail because they do not have the cash on hand to meet their immediate financial obligations. They choke out.

It's like drowning 3 inches below the surface.

In this video, we’re diving into the five most common causes of cash crunches and the steps you should take to avoid them.

We’ll cover:
Five reasons cash flow problems usually start, and how to prevent them from happening to your business
How to make a cash flow plan using our free template
How to use cash flow forecasting to avoid seasonal cash crunches
How to optimize your payment terms to minimize risk to your business
The importance of accounts receivable and timely invoicing to maintaining a healthy cash flow
Why rapid growth can actually kill a contracting business and the importance of scaling sustainably

00:00 - Intro
02:32 - 1) Poor Cash Flow Planning
03:20 - 2) Seasonality
04:40 - 3) Payment Terms
06:42 - 4) Slow Invoicing or No A/R Systems
08:01 - 5) Rapid Growth
09:26 - Benji's Pro Tips for Avoiding Cash Crunches

Subscribe to Breakthrough Academy to never miss a video! 👇
http://bit.ly/youtubebta

Interested in working with us? Let's chat: https://trybta.com/welcome

Benji's Pro Tips for Avoiding Cash Crunches

fulltitle: 5 Reasons Contractors Run Into Cash Flow Issues

comments.text: Before I listen, cash flow does not = profaribility: when cash revenues are less than cash expenses, when the is a high amount of accounts receivable that cannot be turned into cash because there’s a high default rate on the debt, when there inflation in the economy, cost of goods sold increases, but corresponding price is being charged cannot be increased meaning sale prices  are inelastic. When people run their businesses very sloppily think they’re doing well, but spend too much or pay themselves a higher salary and they don’t know the difference between a balance sheet and a cash flow statement. productivity declines the corresponding charges cannot be increased in the marketplace. There is rampant cash theft within a business that goes on for a year or more?
comments.author: @davidvolland7250

comments.text: There wasnt a single breakthrough piece of information in the video. Just  obvious things.  You clickbaited me into watching. Theres your view. Im outta here.
comments.author: @joelduffin7996
